result.chapter_summary: Chapter 2 argues that the decline of American education is the result of an 'anti-curriculum movement' that took hold between 1930 and 1950. This movement, while originating from a valid empathy for children, ultimately crippled schools by rejecting specific grade-by-grade subject matter in favor of the 'magical thinking' that knowledge would develop naturally through experience.

claims.text: The geographical ignorance displayed by modern Americans is primarily the fault of the educational system rather than individual failings.
claims.source_passage: Kellie Pickler presumably attended her local elementary school, so her geographical ignorance was probably not mainly her fault. It is plausible to lay most of the blame on the new ideas that started in a few model schools at the beginning of the twentieth century and gradually took over American elementary schools

claims.text: Progressive educational ideas gradually took over American elementary schools between 1930 and 1950 and have fully dominated them since.
claims.source_passage: new ideas that started in a few model schools at the beginning of the twentieth century and gradually took over American elementary schools between 1930 and 1950, fully dominating them thereafter.

claims.text: Early progressive schools between 1898 and 1918 combined child-centered sympathy with a well-defined academic curriculum designed to produce high-minded citizens.
claims.source_passage: the new ideas and methods were presented as big improvements over educational practices of the past, and in some respects they were—especially in the very early progressive schools at the beginning of the twentieth century, when a newfound sympathy for childhood and the child’s interests was put into the service of delivering a well-defined academic curriculum

claims.text: The fatal flaw of the progressive movement was its faith that required knowledge would develop naturally without a defined academic curriculum.
claims.source_passage: Its fatal flaw was its faith that the knowledge Americans need would naturally develop when the child became fully engaged in concrete experiences without the encumbrance of a defined academic curriculum.

claims.text: The progressive movement created a false dichotomy by equating a set curriculum with harsh and inhumane teaching methods.
claims.source_passage: In the partisan enthusiasm of the new dispensation, educational doctrines became black or white. A set curriculum became equated with harsh and inhumane teaching methods.

claims.text: The progressive movement's defining characteristic was not its focus on the child, but its abandonment of a coherent academic curriculum.
claims.source_passage: the most useful way of viewing the theories that transformed and badly weakened American public education is not by their self-description as “child-centered" but rather by their abandonment of a coherent academic curriculum.

claims.text: In practice, the progressive movement focused more on abolishing traditional academic subjects than on the individual child.
claims.source_passage: In actual practice, the movement’s chief emphasis was less on the individual child, who often got lost in the fads of the day, than on abolishing traditional academic subjects.

claims.text: Expecting high academic achievement from students who have been deprived of a coherent academic curriculum is a form of magical thinking.
claims.source_passage: Their fierce refusal to make an accommodation to definite subject matter now seems a tragic mistake, a kind of magical thinking that expects high academic achievement from students who have been deprived of a coherent academic curriculum.

claims.text: American public elementary schools have lacked a coherent, systematic curriculum for more than half a century.
claims.source_passage: This central historical fact explains how it has happened that for more than half a century our public elementary schools have lacked a coherent curriculum

claims.text: The lack of a coherent curriculum denies children a systematic introduction to human knowledge during their most teachable years.
claims.source_passage: thus denying children at their most teachable age a systematic introduction to the rich domains of human knowledge.

claims.text: The chief American educational movement of the twentieth century is an anti-curriculum movement.
claims.source_passage: The chief American educational movement of the twentieth century, the movement that came to dominate our elementary schools, is an anti-curriculum movement.

claims.text: Progressivist educators used rhetorical attacks, labeling pro-curriculum proponents as fascists and reactionaries, to marginalize dissent.
claims.source_passage: In response, leading progressivists launched what was to become a characteristic rhetorical response: the pro-curriculum professors were bad people, comparable (in the era of Mussolini and Hitler) to the 'fascist menace.' They were allied with such 'enemies of progress' as 'religious fundamentalists' and other 'reactionaries.'

claims.text: The anti-curriculum movement is inherently undemocratic because it preserves social hierarchies and facilitates tyranny.
claims.source_passage: Michael Demiaskevich, a brilliant Russian émigré, wrote excellent books that showed the anti-curriculum movement to be inherently undemocratic in preserving social hierarchies and in offering a fertile field for tyranny and thought control.

claims.text: The enforcement of ideological orthodoxy within schools of education is a significant national problem.
claims.source_passage: Such determined protection of orthodoxy within the education world is now a problem for the nation, for there are now no longer real warring sects in American schools of education.

claims.text: The dominance of a single educational ideology was caused by the 'fateful coincidence' of the expansion of teacher-training colleges and the rise of anti-curriculum theory at Teachers College.
claims.source_passage: The proliferation of education schools in the 1920s thus came at just the time when the new anti-curriculum theories dominated teaching at Teachers College... This was a fateful coincidence for the future of American education, for it determined that a single orientation of educational thought would thenceforth dominate in our education schools.

claims.text: William Heard Kilpatrick's 'The Project Method' (1918) was the most influential pedagogical pamphlet in American history.
claims.source_passage: William Heard Kilpatrick, the author of the most influential pedagogical pamphlet in American educational history—'The Project Method' of 1918.

claims.text: Educational projects are superior to books and lectures according to the progressive pedagogical theory.
claims.source_passage: (Projects are better than books and lectures.)

claims.text: Curriculum revision in American school districts consistently resulted in nearly identical conclusions regardless of local context.
claims.source_passage: In every district regardless of the nature of the community... the deliberations produced nearly identical conclusions. Although there may have been exceptions, there are no reports of curriculum revision in which teachers decided that the theorists were wrong.

claims.text: By the 1970s, the 'open classroom' model became the ultimate expression of anti-curriculum ideas.
claims.source_passage: By the 1940s and 1950s the process was complete, and by the 1970s they had achieved perhaps their ultimate expression in the 'open classroom.'

claims.text: The displacement of an entire national school system's teaching philosophy is a slow process requiring the replacement of several generations of teachers and textbooks.
claims.source_passage: It took a lot of time for the teaching philosophy of an entire national school system to be displaced by a new set of ideas. Incoming teachers, trained in the new philosophy, had to replace older teachers and their ways. New schoolbooks gradually had to replace earlier ones throughout some fifteen thousand school districts.

claims.text: The child-centered, anti-set-curriculum transformation of American schools occurred between the 1930s and the 1950s.
claims.source_passage: The child-centered, anti-set-curriculum transformation of our schools took place over a couple of decades from the 1930s to the 1950s.

claims.text: The full impact of the nationwide implementation of new educational theories in 1950 became visible in high school graduates around 1962.
claims.source_passage: If we take the year 1950 as the approximate date of the full, nationwide implementation of the new theories, we would expect their full impact to be visible in our high school graduates about twelve years later—around 1962.

claims.text: Verbal and math SAT scores of twelfth graders declined precipitously from 1963 to the mid-1980s.
claims.source_passage: And in fact from 1963 to the mid-1980s there was a precipitous decline in the verbal and math SAT scores of twelfth graders.

claims.text: Recent recoveries in math SAT scores are attributable to elementary schools adopting a defined curriculum in math.
claims.source_passage: Under the pressure of public concern, our elementary schools have recently begun to adopt a defined curriculum in math.

claims.text: The lack of recovery in verbal SAT scores is due to the failure to adopt a defined, cumulative curriculum in language arts.
claims.source_passage: But not in language arts. Under the influence of the anti-curriculum movement, an American school finds it almost impossible to deliver a coherent, cumulative curriculum in that central subject.

claims.text: The 'literacy block' in early grades constitutes the greatest squandering of school time due to its focus on trivial stories and drills.
claims.source_passage: The greatest squandering of school time in the early grades occurs in language arts, during the so-called literacy block—the two to three hours spent every day in pursuit of the ever-receding hope that trivial stories and mind-numbing drills will offer a shortcut around the need for the broad general knowledge required for progress in verbal ability.

claims.text: Current language-arts textbooks discourage coherent patterns of instruction by presenting disconnected and trivial reading selections.
claims.source_passage: The available language-arts textbooks actually discourage a coherent pattern of instruction.

claims.text: The scientific literacy of American fifteen-year-olds dropped from seventh to fifteenth out of seventeen countries between the late 1970s and 1988.
claims.source_passage: The study concerned the effectiveness of science education in seventeen countries and was a follow-up to a study undertaken some ten years earlier... 'The United States has dropped from seventh out of seventeen countries to third from the bottom.'

claims.text: As of 2006, the United States ranked twenty-first out of thirty developed nations in scientific literacy among fifteen-year-olds.
claims.source_passage: In a study conducted by the OECD, an organization of developed nations, the United States ranks twenty-first out of thirty countries in the scientific literacy of fifteen-year-olds.

claims.text: The educational concept of 'growth' and 'development' is based on a false metaphor that learning occurs naturally like biological maturation.
claims.source_passage: Similarly, the word growth, which filled the new theorists’ writings, suggested that learning occurs naturally, as a flower develops from a seed.

claims.text: Reading and writing are not natural biological developments but cultural inventions that arose for most people only with universal schooling in the nineteenth century.
claims.source_passage: Never mind that reading and writing did not arise from the nature of most people until well into the nineteenth century, when universal schooling came into being.

claims.text: The concept of 'reading readiness' is a flawed analogy to developmental milestones like walking or talking.
claims.source_passage: Educators began to speak of “reading readiness” as if readiness for reading were analogous to developmental readiness for walking and talking.

claims.text: The phrase 'developmentally appropriate practice' conceives of learning as an unfolding of the child’s nature according to its own laws.
claims.source_passage: Development means unfolding; appropriate means suitable to the individual. The ideal of learning is thus an unfolding of the child’s nature according to its own laws.

claims.text: The use of the word 'growth' in educational theory suggests that learning occurs naturally, analogous to a flower developing from a seed.
claims.source_passage: Similarly, the word growth, which filled the new theorists’ writings, suggested that learning occurs naturally, as a flower develops from a seed.

claims.text: Educational theorists falsely treat 'reading readiness' as a natural developmental milestone analogous to walking or talking.
claims.source_passage: Educators began to speak of “reading readiness” as if readiness for reading were analogous to developmental readiness for walking and talking.

claims.text: Place-value notation is a cultural acquisition rather than a natural developmental milestone.
claims.source_passage: Nor did place-value notation arise from the nature of any European until after the tenth century. The ancient Romans were not developmentally ready for modern math.

claims.text: Modern educational 'constructivism' is a relabeled and updated version of the earlier 'activity movement.'
claims.source_passage: This is a relabeled, updated version of the “activity movement, “under which academic gains were supposed to arise from using pots and pans and engaging in other concrete activities rather than from passive book learning.

claims.text: Mainstream cognitive scientists do not agree with the pedagogical versions of constructivism promoted in schools.
claims.source_passage: Mainstream cognitive scientists do not agree with these relabeled versions of progressivism.

claims.text: Cognitive science has established that understood meaning is something the mind actively constructs rather than passively receives.
claims.source_passage: Ever since Frederic Bartlett’s 1932 book on the constructed character of memory, cognitive scientists have known that understood meaning is something the mind actively constructs rather than passively receives.

claims.text: The scientific principle that knowledge is constructed does not imply that children construct knowledge best through concrete activities rather than verbal communication.
claims.source_passage: The scientific principle does not imply that children construct knowledge best out of concrete activities rather than out of verbal communications. And it turns out they do not.

claims.text: Indirect, activity-based methods do not produce sounder or deeper knowledge than a direct academic curriculum.
claims.source_passage: The most consequential flaw of the theory is that indirect, activity methods do not in fact produce sounder or deeper knowledge than a direct academic curriculum does.

claims.text: Ordinary lively lectures paired with systematic rehearsal and review are more effective for inducing knowledge and critical thinking than the activity methods favored by the anti-curriculum movement.
claims.source_passage: The activities favored by the anti-curriculum movement in the early grades turn out to be less effective in inducing knowledge and critical thinking than ordinary lively lectures along with systematic rehearsal and review.

claims.text: The underlying principle of modern American educational theory is 'providentialism'—the faith that natural development will automatically lead to academic skill.
claims.source_passage: That principle is providentialism. The new theorists believed that the natural development of the child through concrete projects and activities would providentially lead to academic skill and social integration.

claims.text: Educational providentialism is a secularized version of 18th and 19th-century Romantic thought.
claims.source_passage: These ideas arose in Europe and North America in the eighteenth century and flourished in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries in a wave of thought called the Romantic movement.

claims.text: The anti-curriculum movement displaced religious providence from a transcendent God into the developing nature of the schoolchild.
claims.source_passage: The anti-curriculum movement displaced providence from a transcendent God down into the developing nature of the child.

claims.text: Romanticism is 'spilt religion'—the displacement of religious concepts into the secular sphere where they blur human experience.
claims.source_passage: Romanticism, then, and this is the best definition I can give of it, is ‘spilt religion.’

claims.text: The educational establishment places religious trust in secular processes (child development) that experience shows do not warrant such trust.
claims.source_passage: Hulme here identifies a basic difficulty with providential thought in education: religious trust is being placed in something secular that, as experience shows, does not warrant it.

claims.text: The expectation that academic learning will spontaneously develop out of practical activities is a form of magical thinking.
claims.source_passage: Such “spilt religion” is the source of the magical thinking that confidently expects academic learning to develop out of practical activities.

claims.text: Nature is not a reliable guide for educational outcomes and frequently fails to deliver the expected results.
claims.source_passage: Wordsworth also said, “Nature never did betray the heart that loved her.” But Wordsworth was wrong. Nature betrays all the time.

claims.text: Educators have a professional and moral obligation to make conscious, intelligent choices about subject matter and skills rather than leaving them to nature.
claims.source_passage: The new educational theories handed over to nature those concrete, conscious choices of subject matter and skill that educators have an obligation to make as intelligently and worthily as they can

claims.text: Curricular choices should be based on community needs, mainstream psychological science, and a realistic assessment of human nature.
claims.source_passage: basing them on the needs of the community, on mainstream psychological science, and on a realistic assessment of human nature.

claims.text: The failed faith in natural development is sustained by an intellectual monopoly within teacher training institutions.
claims.source_passage: This failed faith in natural development is sustained, unfortunately, by an intellectual monopoly within our teacher training institutions.

claims.text: Education schools operate less like university departments and more like theological institutes where dissent is viewed as heresy to be suppressed.
claims.source_passage: schools, which are less like university departments than theological institutes where heresy is viewed as an evil that its members have a civic duty to suppress.

claims.text: Education professors view it as their duty to shield prospective teachers from exposure to pro-curriculum or heterodox ideas.
claims.source_passage: It is the duty of the education professor to protect prospective teachers from exposure to their ideas.

claims.text: The educational establishment characterizes subject-matter-oriented proponents as authoritarian, undemocratic, and right-wing by definition.
claims.source_passage: Subject-matter-oriented people are by definition authoritarian, undemocratic, and right-wing.

claims.text: Present-day education schools exhibit totalitarian features in their selection and exclusion of course materials.
claims.source_passage: This totalitarian feature of present-day education schools was demonstrated in a data-rich article by David Steiner and Susan Rozen analyzing the syllabi of education courses.

claims.text: The anti-curriculum movement's sense of righteousness and privileged truth often possesses a religious flavor.
claims.source_passage: The anti-curriculum movement’s sense of righteousness, of being in possession of ethical rectitude and privileged truth, often has a religious flavor.

claims.text: The 'Foundations of Education' textbooks uniformly attack content-oriented educational philosophies as reactionary and inhumane.
claims.source_passage: The textbooks available for this course uniformly attack the reactionary and inhumane tendencies of a content-oriented educational philosophy.

claims.text: Education textbooks reduce diverse educational philosophies into a binary of 'child-centered' (humane/modern) versus 'teacher-centered' (inhumane/backward).
claims.source_passage: But, as the textbooks helpfully explain, the theories all really boil down to this: “child-centered” (pro-gressivism and existentialism) versus “teacher-centered” (behaviorism and essentialism). Child-centered theories are modern, forward-looking, and humane, while teacher-centered theories are old-fashioned, backward-looking, and inhumane.

claims.text: The education establishment falsely teaches that progressivism is the sole source of 'general skills' and 'critical thinking' while academic curricula require only 'rote learning.'
claims.source_passage: Currently teachers are being taught that progressivism is motivational and inculcates general skills, independent thought, love of learning, and critical thinking. By contrast, an academic curriculum is anti-motivational, requires rote learning of mere facts, and is antipathetic to independent-mindedness, love of learning, and critical thinking.

claims.text: The classification of teaching philosophies used in teacher training is tendentious and arbitrary.
claims.source_passage: First, the classification of the different teaching philosophies is tendentious and arbitrary.

claims.text: There is no inherent connection between establishing a definite curriculum and any particular form of instruction or classroom management.
claims.source_passage: Second, there is no inherent connection between establishing a definite curriculum and any particular form of instruction and classroom management. This is an absolutely critical point that is universally glossed over in teacher indoctrination.

claims.text: Specific academic topics can be taught using 'lively' and 'humane' methods often associated only with progressivism.
claims.source_passage: There is absolutely no reason why definite topics in world cultures and animal stories (described above as progressivism in the online quiz) cannot be part of a highly explicit multiyear academic curriculum and taught in all the lively ways described.

claims.text: Polarizing slogans act as a propaganda effort to preserve anti-curriculum doctrine despite its practical failure.
claims.source_passage: Perhaps the greatest barrier to dissent is the armory of effective, polarizing slogans that have been erected over the years, a propaganda effort that continues to preserve the anti-curriculum doctrine in the face of failure and decline.

claims.text: Since the 1930s, the anti-curriculum movement has successfully used the rhetorical maneuver of labeling academic curricula as 'right-wing' or 'elitist.'
claims.source_passage: Ever since the 1930s, the identification of a set academic curriculum with an anti-liberal, reactionary, authoritarian, elitist, right-wing point of view has continued to be the most successful and persistent rhetorical maneuver of the anti-curriculum movement.

claims.text: An elementary core curriculum is the essential feature required to achieve the liberal aim of equal educational opportunity.
claims.source_passage: it has influenced major philanthropies to oppose an elementary core curriculum—the feature most needed to carry out the truly liberal aim of greater educational opportunity.

claims.text: Ideological rhetoric in the education world prevents bipartisan agreement on the specifics of the elementary curriculum.
claims.source_passage: the fierce rhetoric of the education world continues to turn the heads of teachers and policy makers alike and to prevent bipartisan agreement on specifics of the elementary curriculum.

claims.text: Cognitive scientists favor an explicit, analytical method of teaching phonics over naturalistic 'whole language' methods.
claims.source_passage: if you favor—as all cognitive scientists do—an explicit, analytical method of teaching children how to turn writing into the sounds of language—phonics—then you are called a conservative, whereas if you favor the naturalistic method of “whole language,” you are a warmhearted, child-centered liberal.

claims.text: Ideological slogans such as 'lock-step education' and 'critical thinking' cause educational issues to be framed in ham-handed ideological terms rather than complex practical ones.
claims.source_passage: Phrases such as “lock-step education,” “rote memorization,” “child-centered,” “whole child,” “social justice,” and “critical thinking” have caused educational issues to be pitched in ham-handed ideological terms rather than in complex practical ones.

claims.text: Pedagogical choices are often polarized by political labels, where favoring phonics is labeled conservative and favoring whole language is labeled liberal.
claims.source_passage: For example, if you favor—as all cognitive scientists do—an explicit, analytical method of teaching children how to turn writing into the sounds of language—phonics—then you are called a conservative, whereas if you favor the naturalistic method of “whole language,” you are a warmhearted, child-centered liberal.

claims.text: An effective core curriculum in the early grades must take a traditional flavor because the foundations of mass literacy change slowly.
claims.source_passage: An effective core curriculum in the early grades—the chief practical need of American elementary education—must necessarily take a somewhat traditional flavor, if only because the foundations of mass literacy are very slow to change.

claims.text: The widespread use of the written word exerts a strong conservative influence on standard national languages and their correlative knowledge.
claims.source_passage: Sociolinguists have shown that the widespread use of the written word has exercised a strong conservative influence on standard national languages and their correlative knowledge.

claims.text: It is impossible to specify effective, literacy-enhancing content in early grades without including traditional, commonly shared elements.
claims.source_passage: It is impossible to specify effective, literacy-enhancing content in the early grades without specifying traditional, commonly shared elements.

claims.text: Any curriculum that successfully enhances literacy can be portrayed as right-wing simply by pointing out topics it excludes.
claims.source_passage: It is therefore all too easy to portray any curriculum that effectively enhances literacy as being right-wing simply by pointing out that it has failed to include particular topics.

claims.text: Anti-curriculum rhetoric uses the inherent vulnerability of a core curriculum to charges of reactionary ideology to prevent the development of any definite curriculum at all.
claims.source_passage: The inherent vulnerability of any effective core curriculum to the charge of promoting a reactionary ideology is thus a powerful tool for preventing the development of any definite core curriculum at all. That is of course the aim— all too successfully achieved—of anti-curriculum rhetoric.

claims.text: Content-based early schooling is the only viable method to promote universal competence and equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: Shall we have content-based early schooling that promotes universal competence and equal opportunity, or shall we have a nonspecific noncurriculum based on the providential, how-to theory of education that has not worked and cannot work?

claims.text: The charter school movement as a whole has been inhospitable to the idea of a common core curriculum across schools.
claims.source_passage: But the movement as a whole has been inhospitable to the idea of a common core curriculum across schools, and many charter schools, like their regular-school counterparts, lack a definite or coherent curriculum.

claims.text: The overall performance of charter schools has not been significantly better than that of ordinary public schools.
claims.source_passage: Charter schools taken as a group have not performed much better than ordinary schools.

claims.text: The disappointments of the charter school movement result from technically ill-informed applications of free-market theory that ignore the specific democratic purposes of schooling.
claims.source_passage: The disappointments of the charter-school movement have been largely due to indiscriminate, technically ill-informed applications of free-market theory.

claims.text: The premises of free-market theory—experimentation, competition, incentives, and choice—are productive forces in human affairs.
claims.source_passage: I agree that the premises of free-market theory are applicable to schools. Experimentation, competition, incentives, and choice are productive forces in human affairs.

claims.text: Accountability is a positive attribute for educational systems.
claims.source_passage: Accountability is a good thing.

claims.text: Local administration is typically more efficient and responsive than centralized bureaucracy.
claims.source_passage: Local administration that stays closer to the realities of an enterprise is usually more nimble and efficient than a centralized bureaucracy.

claims.text: Free-market principles in education must be applied with deep knowledge of schooling's multiple purposes in a democracy.
claims.source_passage: But they need to be applied with deep knowledge of schools and of the multiple purposes of schooling in a democracy.

claims.text: The 'black box' approach of economists avoids central planning defects but often amounts to intellectual laziness.
claims.source_passage: This Olympian perspective is thought to be a virtue in that it avoids the well-known defects of detailed central planning. But it can also be viewed as intellectual laziness.

claims.text: Effective school reform requires substantive knowledge regarding the best methods and aims of schooling.
claims.source_passage: Effective school reform needs substantive knowledge about the best methods and aims of schooling.

claims.text: Most parents lack the specific knowledge of cognitive science required to judge effective schooling.
claims.source_passage: Despite parents’ existential interest in the matter, they often know as little about what is needed for effective schooling as theoretical economists do.

claims.text: Cognitive science indicates that only a cumulative, multiyear approach to schooling is effective.
claims.source_passage: Many expect short-term gains, whereas cognitive science says that only a cumulative, multiyear approach can work.

claims.text: Individual parental choices do not necessarily result in the public good.
claims.source_passage: Theorists of the choice movement seem to adopt the optimistic faith that when a lot of parents make individual choices for their children, that will also be good for the public weal... Not necessarily.

claims.text: The public good must be considered independently of individual preferences in education policy.
claims.source_passage: The public good needs to be considered in its own right.

claims.text: A shared public sphere and vibrant economy are collective interests served by the school system.
claims.source_passage: We all have a stake in promoting an effective public sphere and more vibrant economy through our schools.

claims.text: The public sphere cannot function unless all citizens are educated to participate in it.
claims.source_passage: But the public sphere cannot exist as a democratic vehicle for everyone unless everyone is schooled to participate in it.

claims.text: To support social justice aims, the left needs to promote rather than attack a common core curriculum in early grades.
claims.source_passage: In order to support its social justice aims, the left needs to promote rather than attack a common core curriculum in early grades.

claims.text: The majority of parents support the implementation of a definite core curriculum in elementary schools.
claims.source_passage: When parents are asked if they think there should be a definite core curriculum in elementary schools, they generally say yes.

claims.text: The school-choice movement provides school options but fails to provide the option of a common, coherent curriculum.
claims.source_passage: When the school-choice movement focuses sharply on expanding parents’ options, it is concerned with the freedom to choose different schools, but it does not offer parents the possibility of a common, coherent curriculum.

claims.text: Charter schools are trapped within the larger educational system of fragmented textbooks and inadequately trained teachers.
claims.source_passage: Because of the movement’s lack of consensus about curriculum, charter schools must use the same fragmented textbooks as regular public schools and must draw on the same pool of teachers, who, through no fault of their own, have been offered inadequate subject-matter training by schools of education.

claims.text: The individualistic approach of free-market school theory does not solve the problem of student mobility.
claims.source_passage: As a further practical point, there is nothing so far in the too individualistic approach to free-market school theory that would solve the problem of student mobility.

claims.text: Student mobility rates are significantly higher in inner-city schools than in suburban schools.
claims.source_passage: Mobility rates— the percentages of students who move in and out of schools each year—range between 25 and 40 percent for suburban schools and between 45 and 80 percent for inner-city schools.

claims.text: Over 70 percent of low-income students have attended two schools by the third grade.
claims.source_passage: more than 70 percent of low-income students have attended two schools by third grade!

claims.text: Focusing solely on parental choice and individual schools ignores the needs of low-income students who must move frequently for economic reasons.
claims.source_passage: A focus on parental choice and on the individual school glosses over the plight of those neediest students, who are forced to change schools in large numbers because their parents must move for economic reasons.

claims.text: Charter schools would perform better if all public schools were required to meet definite core curricular standards.
claims.source_passage: In short, existing charter schools would perform much better and competition between schools would be easier to judge if all American public schools of all types had to meet definite core curricular standards—openly arrived at—to build the public sphere.

claims.text: Every school, whether charter or traditional, requires a coherent, definite, grade-by-grade core curriculum to be effective.
claims.source_passage: Charter or not, a good school needs a coherent, definite, grade-by-grade core curriculum.

claims.text: Free-market educational theory has failed to account for the substantive realities and inherent constraints of the education 'industry.'
claims.source_passage: it is because good free-market theory hasn’t paid enough attention to the inherent constraints, goals, and substantive realities of the “industry” in which it operates.

claims.text: The charter-school movement's opposition to a common core curriculum aligns it with the anti-curriculum movement that caused American educational decline.
claims.source_passage: Continued opposition of charter-school theorists to a common core curriculum for all schools in the early grades means that the parental-choice movement has itself acquiesced in the anti-curriculum movement that caused our educational decline.

claims.text: Left-wing education reformers have been as complicit as free-market reformers in perpetuating anti-curriculum dogma.
claims.source_passage: Education reformers on the left, opposed to charters, have just as fervently resisted making definite curricular decisions for early grades and have been just as complicit in perpetuating anti-curriculum dogma.

claims.text: The New Left is 'particularistic' and prioritizes group status over universal education.
claims.source_passage: The New Left has been particularistic, not universalistic. Its highest priority has been to secure recognition and status for women, blacks, Hispanics, and certain other groups.

claims.text: New Left theorists view top-down curricular standards as a plot to maintain a dominant white, male, bourgeois power structure.
claims.source_passage: Education professors eagerly joined New Left professors to promote the idea that any top-down imposition of any curriculum would be a right-wing plot designed to perpetuate the dominant white, male, bourgeois “power structure.”

claims.text: The assumption that a diversity of identity-based curricula leads to higher educational achievement has no basis in evidence.
claims.source_passage: This assumption of a pre-established harmony between group recognition and good educational outcomes makes no sense and has no basis in evidence.

claims.text: Only shared knowledge makes communication and learning possible within a public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Shared knowledge alone enables communication and learning to occur in the public sphere.

claims.text: To achieve its social justice goals, the political Left must promote a common core curriculum.
claims.source_passage: In order to support its social justice aims, the left needs to promote rather than attack a common core curriculum in early grades.

claims.text: The progressive education movement's rejection of curriculum leads to increased social injustice.
claims.source_passage: During the 1920s and 1930s a key insight of left-leaning critics of the anti-curriculum movement was their perception that the new “progressive” movement would lead to ever greater social injustice.

claims.text: The assumption of a pre-established harmony between group recognition and good educational outcomes has no basis in evidence.
claims.source_passage: This assumption of a pre-established harmony between group recognition and good educational outcomes makes no sense and has no basis in evidence.

claims.text: Shared knowledge alone enables communication and learning to occur in the public sphere.
claims.source_passage: Shared knowledge alone enables communication and learning to occur in the public sphere.

claims.text: All anti-core-curriculum movements, whether from the left or right, have resulted in educational failure.
claims.source_passage: The educational failure of all anti-core-curriculum movements, left or right, may offer hope that the overarching goal of social justice might lure all parties away from anti-core-curriculum thinking.

claims.text: Anti-curriculum movements are destined to perpetuate and crystallize social differences.
claims.source_passage: In his Prison Notebooks, he said that the new (anti-curriculum) movement is “advocated as being democratic, while in fact it is destined not merely to perpetuate social differences but to crystallise them in Chinese complexities.”

claims.text: Poor people must master traditional tools of discourse and learning to create a counter hegemony.
claims.source_passage: He explicitly stated that poor people must master the traditional tools of discourse and of learning if they want to create a “counter hegemony.”

claims.text: Effective discourse depends on mastery of knowledge prerequisites that make communication possible, not just the externals of language.
claims.source_passage: effective discourse depends on a mastery not only of the externals of language but also of the knowledge prerequisites that make effective communication possible.

claims.text: The New Left's substitution of group identity for individual identity is intellectually and ethically suspect.
claims.source_passage: Especially suspect intellectually and ethically is the substitution of group identity for individual identity, though so many of us are hybrids, not pure blacks or Hispanics, or even pure male or female.

claims.text: The curricular views of the New Left have failed to raise the economic status of marginalized groups.
claims.source_passage: But the curricular views of the New Left have done little to promote the general welfare, raise the economic status of blacks and Hispanics, or provide the basis for effective job retraining in the information economy.

claims.text: Advocating anti-core-curriculum ideas ensures a continued need for affirmative action by militating against equal opportunity.
claims.source_passage: So long as it continues to advocate its anti-core-curriculum ideas, which militate against equal educational opportunity, it will ensure the continued need for affirmative action.

claims.text: Eighty percent of teachers and citizens believe a common core curriculum for early grades will improve schooling.
claims.source_passage: Surveys show that 80 percent of both teachers and ordinary citizens believe that a common core curriculum for early grades will greatly improve schooling.

claims.text: The term 'providence' etymologically derives from the Latin 'pro-videre,' which specifically means to see ahead and ensure the future follows a plan.
claims.source_passage: Providence, from pro-videre, to see ahead, refers to God’s ability to see the future and make sure that it works out according to his plan.

claims.text: The general public's understanding of the word 'curriculum' correctly implies a predetermined subject matter in history, science, language, and the arts.
claims.source_passage: But the general public believes with good reason that the word curriculum implies predetermined subject matter in history, science, language, and the arts.

claims.text: In a technical sense, every school possesses a curriculum, even if it is fragmented, impoverished, or lacks set subject matter.
claims.source_passage: In that sense every school has a curriculum, however fragmented or impoverished it might be.

claims.text: The 'black-box' approach of economists toward school reform can be viewed as intellectual laziness because it avoids the detailed substantive knowledge required for success.
claims.source_passage: This Olympian perspective is thought to be a virtue in that it avoids the well-known defects of detailed central planning. But it can also be viewed as intellectual laziness.

claims.text: The intense focus on race and gender identity in education may have been a temporary historical necessity for improving the social status of marginalized groups.
claims.source_passage: Perhaps race and gender overkill was a temporary necessity to achieve higher social status for low-status groups.

claims.text: Modern teacher training involves the use of 'decontaminated textbooks' that prevent students from encountering heterodox ideas outside of a controlled environment.
claims.source_passage: Their writings must not be assigned, and if their ideas are mentioned, it must be in the controlled environment of a properly decontaminated textbook.

claims.text: Improving an individual school (like a charter) without addressing the broader curricular system is analogous to building a better car while the roads are full of potholes.
claims.source_passage: Building a marginally better car isn’t very useful when the roads are full of potholes.

claims.text: The lack of modern mathematical knowledge in ancient Rome proves that academic proficiency is a cultural acquisition rather than a biological developmental stage.
claims.source_passage: Nor did place-value notation arise from the nature of any European until after the tenth century. The ancient Romans were not developmentally ready for modern math.

claims.text: Education schools function like theological institutes in that they view dissent as a heresy that must be actively suppressed for civic duty.
claims.source_passage: I am still stunned when I think about how students are being shielded from heterodox ideas in education schools, which are less like university departments than theological institutes where heresy is viewed as an evil that its members have a civic duty to suppress.

evidence.description: An anecdote regarding celebrity Kellie Pickler's inability to identify Hungary as a country or Budapest as its capital on a game show.
Diane Ravitch's historical analysis stating that progressive theories enjoyed a golden age at specific schools between 1898 and 1918.
A 1939 talk by Isaac Kandel which identifies the progressive movement's core attack as being directed against 'subject matter' and 'planned organization of a curriculum.'
Historical growth of normal schools into teachers colleges (88 schools) between 1910-1930.
Description of an NYC open classroom in 1971 showing a lack of desks and unstructured learning environments.
evidence.source_reference: Walter and Miriam Schneir, New York Times Magazine (1971)
evidence.source_passage: What is most striking is that there are no desks for pupils or teachers. Instead, the room is arranged as a workshop. Carelessly draped over the seat, arm, and back of a big old easy chair are three children, each reading to himself.

evidence.description: Diane Ravitch's historical account of curriculum revision processes in American school districts.
SAT verbal and math scores from 1965 to 2005 showing a steep decline beginning in 1963.
The table of contents of a best-selling first-grade reading program listing trivial, disconnected story titles.
A 1988 follow-up study on science education effectiveness in seventeen countries.
evidence.source_reference: International Association for the Evaluation of Educational Achievement (1988)

evidence.description: A 2006 international study of science literacy across thirty countries.
Historical observation that Roman civilization lacked place-value notation despite being 'developed' adults.
Historical timeline showing that literacy was not universal until the 19th century and universal schooling.
Historical delay in the European adoption of place-value notation (post-10th century) as proof that math skills are cultural, not natural.
Frederic Bartlett's 1932 research on memory showing that meaning is actively constructed.
Comparison of activity-based methods versus direct instruction/lectures in early grades (Note 20).
William Wordsworth's poetry in 'The Prelude' illustrating the 'naturalized faith' in wandering guides.
T.E. Hulme's critique of Romanticism as 'spilt religion'.
Hirsch's personal experience at the University of Virginia where education faculty warned students against taking his course.
David Steiner and Susan Rozen's data-rich analysis of education course syllabi.
Analysis of education course syllabi demonstrating a lack of exposure to diverse (pro-curriculum) ideas.
The online quiz 'What Philosophy Is This?' which codes specific classroom behaviors as either 'Progressivism' (positive) or 'Essentialism' (negative).
evidence.source_reference: Myra Sadker and David Sadker, Teachers, Schools, and Society

evidence.description: The historical association of academic curricula with right-wing politics beginning in the 1930s.
Scientific consensus on the effectiveness of phonics compared to whole language.
Sociolinguistic research showing the conservative effect of written language on national standards.
The existence of several hundred Core Knowledge charter schools as evidence of the movement's openness to certain innovations.
Aggregate performance data showing charter schools do not outperform traditional schools on average.
Survey data showing parental support for a core curriculum in elementary schools.
Statistics on mobility rates in suburban schools (25-40%) versus inner-city schools (45-80%).
Statistics showing that 24% of all third graders and 70% of low-income students have attended two schools by third grade.
Anecdotal evidence from a conversation with an intellectual leader of the charter-school movement regarding 'black boxes' and the lack of substantive discussion about schooling details.
In Ireland, the school choice model is paired with a common core curriculum in the early grades.
State language-arts standards often abdicate curricular content by telling students to learn from their 'own cultures and developing viewpoints.'
Antonio Gramsci, William Bagley, and Isaac Kandel predicted that the anti-curriculum movement would increase social injustice.
Surveys of teachers and ordinary citizens regarding a common core curriculum.
Antonio Gramsci's writings on the common school and social differences.

concepts.author_definition: The dominant 20th-century educational movement in America which, under the guise of being child-centered, focused on abolishing traditional academic subjects and pre-ordained subject matter.
A systematic, grade-by-grade introduction to specific subject matter in history, science, language, and the arts that is fixed in advance.
concepts.common_alternative: A fragmented or impoverished set of activities based on immediate child interests or 'skills.'

concepts.author_definition: The irrational expectation that students will achieve high academic results and gain broad knowledge without being taught a specific, coherent curriculum.
A dissident group at Teachers College in the 1930s who believed certain specific subject-matter content was essential to an effective American education.
A 1970s pedagogical arrangement without desks where the room is a workshop and children move freely between activities, representing the extreme of the anti-curriculum ideology.
A teaching philosophy where learning is viewed as an unfolding of the child's nature according to its own laws, rather than the transmission of specific cultural knowledge.
concepts.common_alternative: A standard pedagogical approach focused on matching instruction to a child's cognitive stage.
concepts.source_passage: Development means unfolding; appropriate means suitable to the individual. The ideal of learning is thus an unfolding of the child’s nature according to its own laws.

concepts.author_definition: A two to three hour period in the daily school schedule purportedly for reading instruction that, in practice, focuses on trivial stories and skills-drills instead of content knowledge.
The scientifically flawed belief that a child must reach a certain biological developmental stage before they can learn to read, analogous to walking.
concepts.source_passage: Educators began to speak of “reading readiness” as if readiness for reading were analogous to developmental readiness for walking and talking.

concepts.author_definition: The belief that children must build knowledge through concrete activities rather than verbal instruction, often used to justify a non-academic curriculum.
concepts.common_alternative: The psychological theory that learners activey build their own understanding.
concepts.source_passage: This implies that the scientific way to educate is not to tell children dead facts but to place them in situations that will enable them to construct knowledge on their own.

concepts.author_definition: A secularized Romantic faith that a 'natural' process (like a child's activity) will automatically ensure a successful academic and social outcome without external curriculum guidance.
concepts.source_passage: The new theorists believed that the natural development of the child through concrete projects and activities would providentially lead to academic skill and social integration.

concepts.author_definition: The displacement of religious concepts and trust into the secular sphere where they do not belong, resulting in the romanticization of human nature.
The total control of teacher-training institutions by a single ideology that suppresses dissent and shields students from alternative viewpoints.
A standard course for prospective teachers that Hirsch argues functions as a vehicle for ideological indoctrination against subject-matter curricula.
A reductive binary used by education textbooks to categorize and moralize different educational philosophies, labeling content-based education as 'teacher-centered' and therefore 'inhumane.'
A philosophy characterized by textbooks as humane, motivational, and focused on 'stories' and 'general skills,' as opposed to 'texts' and 'rote facts.'
The slow rate of change in the foundations of mass literacy and standard national languages due to the stabilizing influence of the written word.
Polarized, ideological language (e.g., 'lock-step,' 'child-centered') used to prevent the specification of definite grade-by-grade content.
An approach emphasizing experimentation, competition, incentives, and choice as the primary drivers of school improvement.
concepts.common_alternative: A model based on centralized bureaucratic management and standardized inputs.

concepts.author_definition: A meta-theory that focuses on manipulating external conditions (choice, competition, accountability) while ignoring the internal details and substantive methods of actual schooling.
The collective good of the community and nation, which Hirsch argues is distinct from and not automatically served by individual parental choices.
The frequency with which students change schools; a phenomenon that necessitates a common curriculum to prevent educational gaps for moving students.
A universalistic political orientation that focuses on commonalities and shared public knowledge as a means to achieve social justice.
A particularistic political movement that prioritizes the status and recognition of specific identity groups (racial, gendered, ethnic) over common standards.
A pedagogical approach where schooling is tailored to the specific cultural or racial identity of the student rather than a shared national core.
The creation of an alternative social and political order by marginalized groups, which requires mastery of traditional tools of discourse.
A 'single type' of common school advocated by Gramsci for all students to ensure egalitarian outcomes.
The background knowledge necessary for effective communication that goes beyond the mechanical mastery of language.
The Latin root of providence, meaning 'to see ahead,' used to describe the belief that natural processes will automatically lead to the correct goal.
A textbook used in education schools that presents heterodox or pro-curriculum ideas only in a highly controlled and marginalized manner.
A phrase used by Gramsci to describe the way an anti-curriculum education system crystallizes and perpetuates social differences through unnecessary complexity.
The author's metaphor for education schools, emphasizing their intolerance of dissent and their protection of ideological orthodoxy.
The slow rate of change in the foundations of mass literacy which necessitates a traditional flavor in any effective core curriculum.

cases.name: Early Progressive Schools (1898-1918)
cases.description: A brief 'golden age' of schools like the Lab School at the University of Chicago and the Lincoln School at Teachers College. These schools successfully integrated empathy for the child with a definite academic curriculum and community goals.
key_passages: the new theories enjoyed a brief golden age at a handful of schools between 1898 and 1918.

It is hard to imagine better schools than those in the early part of the twentieth century: the old Lab School at the University of Chicago (est. 1896) or the old Lincoln School (est. 1917) at Teachers College of Columbia University.

cases.name: The expansion of American Teachers Colleges (1910-1930)
cases.description: Between 1910 and 1930, 88 normal schools became teachers colleges. These institutions were staffed by faculty trained primarily at Teachers College at Columbia University, ensuring the national spread of a single ideological orientation.
key_passages: Between 1910 and 1930, eighty-eight normal schools in the United States turned into teachers colleges, and these new departments required new faculty.

To fill the demand for personnel they looked principally to the mother of American teacher-training institutions, Teachers College... The proliferation of education schools in the 1920s thus came at just the time when the new anti-curriculum theories dominated teaching at Teachers College.

cases.name: A New York City Third-Grade Open Classroom (1971)
cases.description: A description of a classroom without desks, where children were reading on easy chairs or mattresses, using geo-blocks and magnets, and moving in and out of the room constantly.
key_passages: What is most striking is that there are no desks for pupils or teachers. Instead, the room is arranged as a workshop.
Carelessly draped over the seat, arm, and back of a big old easy chair are three children, each reading to himself.

cases.name: 1971 New York City elementary school
cases.description: A classroom described as a 'workshop' without desks, where children sprawl on mattresses and teachers move between small groups, used to illustrate the full implementation of child-centered philosophy.
key_passages: What is most striking is that there are no desks for pupils or teachers. Instead, the room is arranged as a workshop... Several other children nearby sprawl comfortably on a covered mattress on the floor

cases.name: The 1930s-1950s Curricular Shift
cases.description: The historical period during which American school districts moved away from set curricula toward child-centered theories, resulting in a lag before the impact was seen in test scores.
key_passages: The child-centered, anti-set-curriculum transformation of our schools took place over a couple of decades from the 1930s to the 1950s.

cases.name: The Activity Movement
cases.description: An early 20th-century educational movement that emphasized concrete projects, such as using pots and pans, as the primary vehicle for academic gains, opposing what it termed 'passive book learning.'
key_passages: This is a relabeled, updated version of the “activity movement, “under which academic gains were supposed to arise from using pots and pans and engaging in other concrete activities rather than from passive book learning.

cases.name: University of Virginia School of Education
cases.description: E.D. Hirsch attempted to teach a course on the achievement gap between different racial groups. Despite his status as a best-selling author and high ratings in the Arts and Sciences department, he drew only a handful of students because education faculty explicitly warned students not to attend his 'heretical' class.
key_passages: I was surprised when I drew just a handful—ten or so students and no auditors.
they were all in my class despite having been explicitly warned by members of the education faculty not to take the course.

cases.name: American Education Schools
cases.description: Described as 'totalitarian' institutions that function like theological institutes to ensure future teachers adopt anti-curriculum views. They utilize specific textbooks and courses to frame the debate as a moral struggle between 'humane' progressivism and 'reactionary' essentialism.
key_passages: schools, which are less like university departments than theological institutes where heresy is viewed as an evil
This totalitarian feature of present-day education schools was demonstrated in a data-rich article by David Steiner and Susan Rozen

cases.name: The US Charter School Movement
cases.description: A movement comprising about 1.2 million children in 4,100 schools that offers alternatives to traditional public schools. While the author praises it for allowing Core Knowledge schools to emerge, he characterizes the movement overall as a disappointment because it lacks a common curriculum and shows highly variable performance results.
key_passages: Today about 1.2 million children are students in some 4,100 charter schools.
But the movement as a whole has been inhospitable to the idea of a common core curriculum across schools...
Charter schools taken as a group have not performed much better than ordinary schools.
contestable_aspects: The reason for charter school underperformance (market barriers vs. lack of curriculum)
The characterization of the movement as 'inhospitable' to a common core

cases.name: Ireland
cases.description: An example of a nation where educational competition (school choice) functions effectively because it is underpinned by a common core curriculum in the early grades.
key_passages: School choice works best in places like Ireland, where there is a common core curriculum in the early grades.

thinkers.name: Diane Ravitch
thinkers.key_idea: Progressive education had a short-lived successful period (1898-1918) where child-centeredness coexisted with academic content.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses Ravitch's historical periodization to distinguish between 'good' early progressivism and the 'anti-curriculum' version that followed.
key_passages: As the historian of education Diane Ravitch has pointed out, the new theories enjoyed a brief golden age at a handful of schools between 1898 and 1918.

thinkers.name: Isaac Kandel
thinkers.key_idea: The progressive movement was fundamentally defined by its hostility toward subject matter and planned curriculum organization.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Kandel's 1939 observation as the definitive insight into what went wrong with American schooling, adopting Kandel's focus on the 'anti-subject-matter' nature of the reforms.
key_passages: Kandel is right. The most fruitful way to think about the ideas that took over American elementary schools in the early twentieth century is to focus on this hostility to set subject matter.

thinkers.name: I.L. Kandel
thinkers.key_idea: The lack of coherent, essential subject matter is the core problem in education.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Kandel's 1930s observation as the 'nub of the matter' regarding American education's decline.
key_passages: As Kandel perceived back in the 1930s, that is the nub of the matter.

thinkers.name: Michael Demiaskevich
thinkers.key_idea: The anti-curriculum movement is undemocratic and leads to social hierarchy and tyranny.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch highlights Demiaskevich's work as 'excellent' and 'distinguished,' using his personal tragedy (suicide due to academic isolation) to illustrate the enforcement of ideological orthodoxy in education.
key_passages: Michael Demiaskevich, a brilliant Russian émigré, wrote excellent books that showed the anti-curriculum movement to be inherently undemocratic

thinkers.name: William Heard Kilpatrick
thinkers.key_idea: The Project Method, which prioritizes student projects over books and lectures.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies Kilpatrick as a key figure whose 'Project Method' helped destroy coherent curriculum.
key_passages: William Heard Kilpatrick, the author of the most influential pedagogical pamphlet in American educational history—'The Project Method' of 1918. (Projects are better than books and lectures.)

thinkers.name: Walter and Miriam Schneir
thinkers.key_idea: Proponents of the 'open' or workshop-style classroom which lacks desks and fixed instruction.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes them to provide a vivid, descriptive example of the 'anti-curriculum' environment that replaced traditional schooling.
key_passages: What is most striking is that there are no desks for pupils or teachers. Instead, the room is arranged as a workshop.

thinkers.name: Frederic Bartlett
thinkers.key_idea: The constructed character of memory; understood meaning is actively constructed by the mind.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch accepts Bartlett's scientific findings but argues that they have been misappropriated by educators to justify bad pedagogy.
key_passages: Ever since Frederic Bartlett’s 1932 book on the constructed character of memory, cognitive scientists have known that understood meaning is something the mind actively constructs rather than passively receives.

thinkers.name: William Wordsworth
thinkers.key_idea: The poet expresses a 'naturalized faith' in which a 'wandering cloud' is a sufficient guide, illustrating Romantic trust in nature.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch quotes Wordsworth to illustrate the Romantic intellectual origins of the 'providential' mindset in education.
key_passages: William Wordsworth captures the tone of this naturalized faith: ... and should the chosen guide / Be nothing better than a wandering cloud, / I cannot miss my way.

thinkers.name: T. E. Hulme
thinkers.key_idea: Romanticism is 'spilt religion'—religious concepts poured over the secular sphere, blurring the outlines of experience.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch adopts Hulme's definition of 'spilt religion' as the best way to understand why educators place irrational trust in natural child development.
key_passages: Romanticism, then, and this is the best definition I can give of it, is ‘spilt religion.’

thinkers.name: David Steiner and Susan Rozen
thinkers.key_idea: Syllabi in education courses demonstrate a lack of intellectual diversity and a focus on specific ideological viewpoints.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites their data-rich article to support his characterization of education schools as 'totalitarian' and ideologically closed.
key_passages: This totalitarian feature of present-day education schools was demonstrated in a data-rich article by David Steiner and Susan Rozen

thinkers.name: Myra Sadker and David Sadker
thinkers.key_idea: Educational philosophies can be categorized and tested through simple classroom scenarios (Progressivism vs. Essentialism).
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses their textbook and associated online test as a prime example of the 'dishonest trick' played on teachers to make them view academic curricula as inhumane.
key_passages: I took an online test associated with one of these textbooks, Teachers, Schools, and Society, by Myra Sadker and David Sadker.
A dishonest trick is being played on our prospective teachers.

thinkers.name: Unnamed Charter-School Leader (Economist)
thinkers.key_idea: The belief that economists appropriately treat schooling as a 'black box,' manipulating structures rather than concerning themselves with substantive educational details.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch uses this conversation to illustrate the 'intellectual laziness' and practical inadequacy of ignoring educational content in reform theory.
key_passages: I complained that the manifestos of the program were all about theoretical structure, with little substantive discussion about the details of good schooling... He smiled at me sympathetically. 'But, Don, that’s what economists do. They manipulate black boxes.'

thinkers.name: Albert Shanker
thinkers.key_idea: Universalistic union leadership and commitment to the 'Old Left' ideals of common schooling.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch identifies with Shanker's universalism and uses his biography to contrast the Old Left with the New Left.
key_passages: I, like Al, belong to the universalistic (and better) Old Left.

thinkers.name: Antonio Gramsci
thinkers.key_idea: Progressive education would lead to greater social injustice for the marginalized.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch cites Gramsci as a leftist thinker who correctly foresaw that the lack of a rigorous, common curriculum would harm the social groups it intended to help.
key_passages: Notable critics making this all-too-accurate prediction were the great Italian leftist thinker Antonio Gramsci

thinkers.name: William Bagley
thinkers.key_idea: Criticized the anti-curriculum movement from a left-leaning perspective.
thinkers.engagement: Cited alongside Gramsci and Kandel as a professor who predicted the failure of progressivism in education.
key_passages: Notable critics making this all-too-accurate prediction were... two left-leaning professors from Teachers College, William Bagley and Isaac Kandel.

thinkers.name: Jürgen Habermas
thinkers.key_idea: The necessity of a common public sphere.
thinkers.engagement: Hirsch notes that the New Left invokes Habermas but ignores the requirement of a common curriculum to actually create the public sphere he describes.
key_passages: The New Left often invokes the philosophy of Jürgen Habermas but ignores the inconvenient implications of his plea for a common public sphere

actors.name: Kellie Pickler
actors.context: Used as a modern example of the geographical ignorance produced by the American school system.

actors.name: Diane Ravitch
actors.context: Historian of education whose timeline of progressive education Hirsch adopts.

actors.name: Isaac Kandel
actors.context: A 1930s critic of progressive education whose analysis of the movement's anti-subject-matter bias Hirsch validates.

actors.name: Teachers College, Columbia University
actors.context: Described as the 'mother of American teacher-training institutions' and the source of the anti-curriculum orthodoxy.

actors.name: Essentialists
actors.context: A group of dissidents at Teachers College who opposed the anti-curriculum movement.

actors.name: Professional consultants
actors.context: The agents who led school districts through the curriculum revision process, ensuring alignment with 'modern thinking.'

actors.name: Houghton Mifflin
actors.context: Publisher of the best-selling first-grade reading program critiqued by Hirsch for its trivial content.

actors.name: OECD
actors.context: Organization of developed nations that conducts international science literacy studies showing US decline.

actors.name: College Board
actors.context: Source for the SAT score data used to demonstrate the decline in student achievement.

actors.name: Mainstream cognitive scientists
actors.context: Used to debunk the pedagogical claims of constructivism.

actors.name: Romantic movement
actors.context: The intellectual source of the 'providential' and 'natural growth' ideas in education.

actors.name: University of Virginia School of Education
actors.context: The specific institution where Hirsch experienced ideological suppression.

actors.name: Education Professors
actors.context: Described as acting like priests in a theological institute who shield students from heresy.

actors.name: Education Professors
actors.context: Accused of acting as guardians of orthodoxy who prevent teachers from being exposed to pro-curriculum ideas.

actors.name: Prospective Teachers
actors.context: The target of ideological indoctrination in 'Foundations of Education' courses.

actors.name: Cognitive Scientists
actors.context: Cited as universally favoring explicit phonics instruction, despite the ideological labeling of phonics as 'conservative.'

actors.name: Cognitive scientists
actors.context: Advocates for explicit phonics instruction.

actors.name: Sociolinguists
actors.context: Researchers who identified the conservative nature of written language.

actors.name: Charter-school movement
actors.context: A movement that has generally ignored the need for a common curriculum despite its market-based successes.

actors.name: Schools of education
actors.context: Institutions seen as perpetuating anti-curriculum doctrines.

actors.name: Charter-school theorists
actors.context: Critiqued for an optimistic faith that individual choices aggregate to the public good.

actors.name: Parents
actors.context: Viewed as having an 'existential interest' in schooling but lacking technical knowledge about cognitive science or curriculum.

actors.name: Schools of Education
actors.context: Accused of providing inadequate subject-matter training to teachers.

actors.name: Charter-school theorists
actors.context: Accused of ignoring the reality of student mobility and the necessity of a core curriculum.

actors.name: New Left professors
actors.context: Promoted the idea that curricula are right-wing power structures.

actors.name: Multicultural movement
actors.context: Collaborated with education schools to replace common standards with identity-based curricula.

actors.name: The New Left
actors.context: Critiqued for its focus on group identity over shared curriculum and for its jargon-ridden, elitist discourse.

actors.name: Isaac Kandel
actors.context: Left-leaning professor who predicted the social injustice resulting from progressive educational reforms.

objections_raised.objection: A set, preordained curriculum is inherently harsh, inhumane, and contrary to the interests of the child.
objections_raised.source: The Progressive Movement (as characterized by Hirsch)
objections_raised.response: This is a false dichotomy; early progressive schools (1898-1918) proved that child-centered teaching methods could be used to deliver a definite, academic curriculum effectively.

objections_raised.objection: It seems odd to call child-centered theory an 'anti-curriculum' movement because every school offers something as a curriculum.
objections_raised.response: While schools technically have a 'curriculum' (however fragmented), the term properly implies 'predetermined subject matter,' which the progressive movement actively rejects.

objections_raised.objection: The decline in SAT scores might be attributed to things other than curriculum (implied by the timeline argument).
objections_raised.response: Hirsch argues the timeline of the curricular shift (1930-1950) matches the 'lag' time required for a student to reach the 12th grade (1962), suggesting a causal link.

objections_raised.objection: Deep learning is fundamentally internal, so teachers cannot help by presenting facts or rules; they must instead create environments for construction.
objections_raised.response: Construction occurs even when receiving verbal communication; direct instruction and systematic review are empirically more effective at inducing knowledge.

objections_raised.objection: The activities-method of schooling is better because it is more natural.
objections_raised.response: Nature is not a reliable guide and 'betrays all the time'; educators have an obligation to make conscious choices based on science and community needs.

objections_raised.objection: A set academic curriculum is inherently 'deadly,' 'inhumane,' and requires students to be 'regimented' into 'rote learning.'
objections_raised.source: Foundations of Education Textbooks (e.g., Sadker & Sadker)
objections_raised.response: There is no inherent link between curriculum content and instructional style. Lively, humane, and child-friendly methods can be used to teach an explicit, multiyear academic curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: Favoring a specific curriculum or phonics instruction is a 'reactionary' or 'right-wing' political stance.
objections_raised.response: This is a rhetorical maneuver used to silence dissent; in fact, a common curriculum is the most effective tool for the 'truly liberal aim' of equal opportunity.

objections_raised.objection: Charter schools haven't succeeded because of artificially induced barriers and start-up pains, not because of the underlying theory.
objections_raised.response: The failure is due to an indiscriminate application of free-market theory that ignores the necessity of a coherent, specific curriculum.

objections_raised.objection: The charter-school theory is correct, but artificially induced barriers and start-up pains have prevented it from working so far; skeptics need more patience.
objections_raised.response: We cannot ethically sacrifice generations of children waiting for a hands-off approach to eventually produce results; reform must be based on substantive knowledge of methods and aims.

objections_raised.objection: Any top-down imposition of a curriculum is a right-wing plot to maintain the power of the dominant white, male, bourgeois class.
objections_raised.response: Identity-based curricula have no basis in evidence for improving outcomes; only shared knowledge allows for the communication necessary to participate in the public sphere and achieve social justice.

objections_raised.objection: Group recognition leads to better educational outcomes.
objections_raised.source: Unattributed (attributed to 'this assumption' in the movement)
objections_raised.response: This assumption makes no sense and has no basis in evidence; only shared knowledge enables learning in the public sphere.
This was a false characterization; their predictions about progressive education causing social injustice were accurate.

cross_chapter_refs.reference_text: As I shall show in the last chapter of this book, the fierce rhetoric of the education world continues to turn the heads of teachers and policy makers alike
There is little anyone can do to speed up this linguistic inertness, as I shall point out in chapter 4, “Linguistic America and the Public Sphere.”
As I shall show in the last chapter of this book, the fierce rhetoric of the education world continues to turn the heads of teachers and policy makers alike...
